PRELIMINARY CONSENT SOLICITATION MATERIALS – SUBJECT TO COMPLETION

 

 

340 Madison Avenue, Suite 3C
New York, New York 10173
(212) 235-2191

 

January [__], 2024

 

Dear Stockholders,

 

We are soliciting consents from the holders of the common stock, par value $0.01 per share (CUSIP No. 89656D101) (the “Common Stock”) of Trinity Place Holdings Inc., a Delaware corporation (the “Company”, “we”, “us” or “our”) to (i) adopt resolutions authorizing the (x) Stock Purchase Agreement (the “Stock Purchase Agreement”), dated as of January 5, 2024, by and among the Company, TPHS Lender LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and the lender under the Company’s corporate credit facility (the “Company Investor”) and TPHS Investor LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and an affiliate of Company Investor (the “JV Investor”, and together with the Company Investor, the “Investor”), pursuant to which the Company Investor will be issued 25,112,245 shares of Common Stock of the Company (the “Investor Shares”) in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, (y) amended and restated limited liability company operating agreement of TPHGreenwich Holdings LLC (the “JV”) to be entered into by the Company and the JV Investor (the “JV Operating Agreement”) at the closing of the transactions contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement, pursuant to which the JV Investor will be appointed the initial manager of, and acquire a five percent (5%) interest in, the JV, which JV will continue to own, indirectly, all of the real property assets of the Company upon the consummation of the transactions contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement and the JV Operating Agreement, and which JV will initially hire a newly formed wholly-owned subsidiary of the Company to act as asset manager for the JV for an annual management fee (collectively, the “Transactions”), and (z) Transactions contemplated thereby, which Transactions may be deemed to be a sale, lease or exchange of all or substantially all of the property and assets of the Company within the meaning of Section 271 of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the “Joint Venture Proposal”) and (ii) approve the issuance of the Investor Shares to the Investor in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, which may be required to comply with (x) Section 713(a) and (y) Section 713(b) of the NYSE American LLC Company Guide (the “Issuance of Common Stock Proposals” and together with the Joint Venture Proposal, the “Stockholder Proposals”).

 

Our Board of Directors (“Board”) has approved the Stock Purchase Agreement, the JV Operating Agreement and the Transactions, subject to the approval of the Company’s stockholders, and considers the above-referenced actions to be desirable to increase the liquidity of the Company and provide the Company additional time and an enhanced structure to explore other potential strategic alternatives. Our Board of Directors recommends that you provide your written consent “FOR” the Stockholder Proposals.

 

The consent solicitation statement (the “Consent Solicitation Statement”) accompanying this letter contains important information about the above referenced actions for which we are seeking your consent. You are urged to read the accompanying consent solicitation statement carefully and in its entirety.

 

Your vote is important no matter how large or small your holdings in the Company may be. Although the Board has approved the Stockholder Proposals, an affirmative vote of the Company’s stockholders holding a majority of the outstanding Common Stock is being sought to authorize and adopt the Stockholder Proposals. Stockholders of record as of the record date of January 2, 2024 are requested to complete, date and sign the enclosed written consent solicitation form and to return it promptly on or before                    , 2024 (or such later date as the Board may determine, with the consent of the Investor) (the “Consent Deadline”) to ensure that their votes are counted with respect to the Stockholder Proposals. If we obtain the required number of consents prior to the Consent Deadline, our Board of Directors may decide to proceed with effecting any of the Stockholder Proposals prior to the Consent Deadline.

 

 

 

If you have any questions about voting of your shares, please contact the Company’s proxy solicitor, D.F. King & Co., Inc., toll-free at (800) 628-8532.

 

Thank you for your investment and continued interest in Trinity Place Holdings Inc.

 

Sincerely,

 

   
Matthew Messinger  
President and Chief Executive Officer  

 

 

 

CAUTIONARY NOTE REGARDING FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

 

This Consent Solicitation Statement may include forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “Exchange Act”), and information relating to us that are based on the beliefs of management as well as assumptions made by and information currently available to management. These forward-looking statements include, but are not limited to, statements about our plans, objectives, expectations and intentions that are not historical facts, and other statements identified by words such as “may,” “will,” “expects,” “believes,” “plans,” “estimates,” “potential,” or “continues,” or the negative thereof or other and similar expressions. In addition, in some cases, you can identify forward-looking statements by words or phrases such as “trend,” “potential,” “opportunity,” “believe,” “comfortable,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “current,” “intention,” “estimate,” “position,” “assume,” “outlook,” “continue,” “remain,” “maintain,” “sustain,” “seek,” “achieve,” and similar expressions. Such statements reflect our current views with respect to future events, the outcome of which is subject to certain risks, including among others: (i) the risk that the Transactions are not successfully or timely consummated, including the risk that the approval of the stockholders of the Company is not obtained or other closing conditions are not satisfied or waived; (ii) the ability of the Company to successfully identify and consummate a TopCo Strategic Transaction (defined below) following the consummation of the Transactions; (iii) the outcome of any legal proceedings that may be instituted against the Company or others following the announcement of the proposed Transactions and any definitive agreements with respect thereto; (iv) the ability to meet stock exchange listing standards at or following the consummation of the proposed Transactions; and (v) the risk that the proposed Transactions disrupt current plans and operations of the Company as a result of the announcement and consummation of the proposed Transactions. In evaluating such statements, you should specifically consider the risks identified under the section entitled “Risk Factors” in our 2022 Annual Report for the year ended December 31, 2022, as filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (the “SEC”) on March 31, 2023, under the section entitled “Risk Factors” and our subsequently filed Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q, and the risks and uncertainties described in subsequent filings with the SEC, any of which could cause actual results to differ materially from the anticipated results.  Should one or more of these risks or uncertainties materialize, or should underlying assumptions prove incorrect, actual results or outcomes may vary materially from those contemplated by any forward-looking statements. Subsequent written and oral forward-looking statements attributable to us or persons acting on our behalf are expressly qualified in their entirety by the cautionary statements in this paragraph and elsewhere described in our 2022 Annual Report and other reports filed with the SEC. All forward-looking statements speak only as of the date hereof, and we assume no obligation to update any forward-looking statements, except as required by law.

 

 

 

 

CONSENT SOLICITATION STATEMENT

 

This consent solicitation statement (the “Consent Solicitation Statement”) contains information as of
January [__], 2024 unless otherwise stated.

 

GENERAL INFORMATION

 

We are soliciting consents from the holders of the common stock, par value $0.01 per share (the “Common Stock”) of Trinity Place Holdings Inc., a Delaware corporation (the “Company”, “we”, “us” or “our”) to (i) adopt resolutions authorizing the (x) Stock Purchase Agreement (the “Stock Purchase Agreement”), dated January 5, 2024, by and among the Company, TPHS Lender LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and the lender under the Company’s corporate credit facility (the “Company Investor”) and TPHS Investor LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and an affiliate of Company Investor (the “JV Investor”, and together with the Company Investor, the “Investor”), pursuant to which the Company Investor will be issued 25,112,245 shares of Common Stock of the Company (the “Investor Shares”) in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, (y) amended and restated limited liability company operating agreement of TPHGreenwich Holdings LLC (the “JV”) to be entered into by the Company and the JV Investor (the “JV Operating Agreement”) at the closing of the transactions contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement (the “Closing”), pursuant to which the JV Investor will be appointed the initial manager of, and acquire a five percent (5%) interest in, the JV, which JV will continue to own, indirectly, all of the real property assets of the Company upon the consummation of the transactions contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement and the JV Operating Agreement, and which JV will initially hire a newly formed wholly-owned subsidiary of the Company to act as asset manager for the JV for an annual management fee (collectively, the “Transactions”), and (z) Transactions contemplated thereby, which Transactions may be deemed to be a sale, lease or exchange of all or substantially all of the property and assets of the Company within the meaning of Section 271 of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the “DGCL”, and such proposal, the “Joint Venture Proposal”) and (ii) approve the issuance of the Investor Shares to the Investor in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, which may be required to comply with (x) Section 713(a) and (y) Section 713(b) of the NYSE American LLC Company Guide (the “Issuance of Common Stock Proposals” and together with the Joint Venture Proposal, the “Stockholder Proposals”).

 

Solicitation of Consents

 

Our Board of Directors (“Board”) has elected to seek approval for the Stockholder Proposals by written consent (“Written Consent”) pursuant to Section 228 of the DGCL and the bylaws of the Company (the “Bylaws”) rather than calling a meeting of our stockholders. We are soliciting consents from all holders of the Company’s Common Stock as of the close of business on January 2, 2024 (the “Record Date”). Our Board does not intend to call a meeting of our stockholders to consider the Stockholder Proposals.

 

Holders of record of our Common Stock as of the Record Date are requested to return their Written Consents to Broadridge Financial Services, Inc. (“Broadridge”) by no later than 4:00 PM New York City Time on                   , 2024 (or such later date as the Board may determine, with the consent of the Investor) (the “Consent Deadline”). Stockholder approval of the Stockholder Proposals will be deemed effective upon the Company receiving written affirmative consents from stockholders holding a majority of the outstanding Common Stock, provided that such Written Consents are received prior to the Consent Deadline. If we do not receive the required number of consents prior to the Consent Deadline, any consents provided will be deemed to have expired.

 

Stockholders wishing to provide their approval to any of the Stockholder Proposals should complete, sign and date the accompanying written consent form (the “Consent Form”) and return it in accordance with the instructions set out therein as soon as possible. Stockholders will be requested to indicate on their Consent Form whether they are “FOR”, “AGAINST” or “ABSTAIN” with respect to the approval of each of the Stockholder Proposals. Stockholders that sign and return a Consent Form but do not indicate a choice on that Consent Form with respect to any of the Stockholder Proposals will be deemed to have consented “FOR” the approval of such action.

 

1

 

 

If the shares of Common Stock to which a Consent Form relates are held by two or more joint Registered Holders, each such Registered Holder must sign the Consent Form. If a signature is by a trustee, executor, administrator, guardian, attorney-in-fact, officer of a corporation or other Registered Holder acting in a fiduciary or representative capacity, such person should so indicate when signing and must submit proper evidence satisfactory to us of such person’s authority to act on behalf of the Registered Holder. If shares of Common Stock are held in different names, a separate Consent Form must be executed covering each name.

 

If a Consent relates to fewer than all of the shares of Common Stock held of record as of the Record Date by the Registered Holder providing such Consent, such Registered Holder must indicate on the Consent Form the aggregate number of shares of Common Stock to which the Consent Form relates. Otherwise, the Consent Form will be deemed to relate to all of the shares of Common Stock registered in the name of the Registered Holder.

 

Vote Sought to Authorize and Adopt the Stockholder Proposals

 

In order for any or all of the Stockholder Proposals to be authorized and adopted, Registered Holders (defined below) holding greater than 50% of the total number of shares of our Common Stock outstanding on the Record Date must provide their Written Consent to the Stockholder Proposals. Each share of our Common Stock is entitled to one “vote” for every share of our Common Stock held of record on the Record Date.

 

Entitlement to Provide Consent

 

At the close of business on the Record Date, there were 38,444,787 shares of Common Stock outstanding (CUSIP No. 89656D101).

 

Only registered holders of record of our Common Stock (“Registered Holders”) on the Record Date are entitled to vote by submitting their Written Consent with respect to any or all of the Stockholder Proposals. Registered Holders of our Common Stock are entitled to one (1) “vote” for every share of Common Stock held of record as of the Record Date. Stockholders entitled to vote by submitting their Written Consent may do so by completing, signing and returning their Consent Form in the manner described under “Solicitation of Consents”.

 

Holders of our Common Stock that hold their shares through an intermediary such as a broker, custodian, commercial bank, trust company or other nominee (an “Intermediary”) will likely not be the holder of record of those shares of Common Stock (a “Non-Registered Holder”). Non-Registered Holders will not be eligible to directly vote or consent to any of the Stockholder Proposals. Instead, it is likely that the Intermediary is the stockholder of record of those shares of Common Stock that are beneficially owned by you. Persons holding shares Common Stock through an Intermediary should contact their Intermediary to inquire as to how to instruct the Intermediary to consent, withhold consent or abstain to the Stockholder Proposals on your behalf. If a holder of our Common Stock requests a printed copy of the consent materials by mail, such holder’s broker will provide a Consent Form for the holder to use.

 

Under the DGCL, Written Consents will remain in effect until a sufficient number of Written Consents are received by us to take the actions proposed herein; provided, however, that we must receive a sufficient number of Written Consents to take the actions proposed herein within 60 days of the first date that a Written Consent is delivered to the Company in accordance with the DGCL.

 

How to Vote

 

Stockholders may generally vote by one of the following methods:

 

•By Mail. You may vote by signing, dating and returning your Written Consent in the enclosed pre-addressed envelope; or
   
•By Methods Listed on the Written Consent. Please refer to your Written Consent or other information provided by your bank, broker, nominee or other stockholder of record to determine whether you may vote electronically on the internet, and follow the instructions on the Written Consent or other information provided to the stockholders of record.

 

2

 

 

If you hold your shares through a broker, bank or other nominee, you are considered the owner of beneficial interests in the Shares (a “beneficial owner”), and your broker is the holder of record. Stockholders should have received instructions on how to vote from your broker, bank or other nominee. Please follow their instructions carefully. You have the right to direct your broker as to how to vote your Shares. If you request a printed copy of the consent materials by mail, your broker will provide a Written Consent for you to use.

 

If you have any questions about this Consent Solicitation Statement, the Stockholder Proposals or the Transactions, or if you need additional copies of this Consent Solicitation Statement or the enclosed Consent Form, you should contact:

 

D.F. King & Co., Inc.

48 Wall Street, 22nd Floor

New York, NY 10005

Telephone: (800) 628-8532

E-mail: TPHS@dfking.com

 

Accessing Consent Solicitation Statement Electronically

 

Stockholders can view this Consent Solicitation Statement on the internet at www.proxyvote.com.

 

Revocation of Consents

 

You may revoke your consent at any time before the earlier of the Consent Deadline and the date on which we receive enough Written Consents to approve any of the Stockholder Proposals. If you are a Registered Holder and you wish to revoke your previously given consent, you may do so by casting another vote by mail or online prior to the Consent Deadline (or such date on which we receive enough Written Consents to approve the Stockholder Proposals) or contacting Broadridge by telephone at its toll-free number at 1-800-690-6903, and following their instructions with respect to the revocation of your consent .

 

If you are a Non-Registered Holder and you wish to revoke your previously given consent, you must contact your Intermediary and follow the instructions provided by them to revoke your consent.

 

Appraisal Rights

 

No dissenters’ or appraisal rights are available under the DGCL or under our Certificate of Incorporation, as amended, as a result of the corporate action referenced herein. This means that no stockholder is entitled to receive any cash or other payment as a result of, or in connection with the corporate action, even if a stockholder has not been given an opportunity to vote.

 

Costs of the Solicitation

 

This solicitation of consents is being made by our Board and we are bearing the costs of such solicitation, including the preparation, printing and mailing costs associated with this solicitation. Written Consents will be solicited primarily through the mails. However, our directors, officers and employees may solicit Written Consents personally or by telephone or other means. Arrangements will be made with brokerage firms and other custodians, nominees and fiduciaries to forward these consent solicitation materials to stockholders who own shares of our Common Stock through such brokerage firms and other custodians, nominees and intermediaries. We will reimburse such brokerage firms and other custodians, nominees and intermediaries for reasonable out-of-pocket expenses incurred in connection therewith. We may also pay for and utilize the services of outside individual or companies that we do not regularly employ in connection with the solicitation of consents for the Stockholder Proposals if our management determines that the engagement of such persons is advisable.

 

The Company has also engaged the services of D.F. King & Co., Inc. for a fee of approximately $15,000, plus reimbursement of expenses, to assist in the solicitation of Written Consents.

 

3

 

 

SECURITY OWNERSHIP OF CERTAIN BENEFICIAL OWNERS AND MANAGEMENT

 

The following table sets forth certain information concerning the number of shares of our Common Stock owned beneficially as of January 2, 2024 by: (i) each person (including any group) known to us to own more than five percent (5%) of any class of our voting securities, (ii) each of our directors and each of our named executive officers (as defined under Item 402(m)(2) of Regulation S-K), and (iii) officers and directors as a group. Unless otherwise indicated, the stockholders listed possess sole voting and investment power with respect to the shares shown. Under Rule 13d-3, a beneficial owner of a security includes any person who, directly or indirectly, through any contract, arrangement, understanding, relationship, or otherwise has or shares: (i) voting power, which includes the power to vote, or to direct the voting of shares; and (ii) investment power, which includes the power to dispose or direct the disposition of shares. Certain shares may be deemed to be beneficially owned by more than one person (if, for example, persons share the power to vote or the power to dispose of the shares). In addition, shares are deemed to be beneficially owned by a person if the person has the right to acquire the shares (for example, upon exercise of an option) within 60 days of the date as of which the information is provided. In computing the percentage ownership of any person, the amount of shares outstanding is deemed to include shares beneficially owned by such person (and only such person) by reason of these acquisition rights. As a result, the percentage of outstanding shares of any person as shown in this table does not necessarily reflect the person’s actual ownership or voting power with respect to the number of shares of Common Stock actually outstanding.

The address of DS Fund is 1001 Brickell Bay Dr., Suite 3102A, Miami, FL 33131.

 

5

 

 

Company Overview

 

The Company is a real estate holding, investment, development and asset management company. The Company is the successor to Syms Corp. (“Syms”), which also owned Filene’s Basement, owners and operators of a chain of “off-price” retail stores under the “Syms” and “Filene’s Basement” names. In September 2012, the Syms Plan of Reorganization (the “Plan”) became effective and Syms and its subsidiaries consummated their reorganization under Chapter 11 through a series of transactions and emerged from bankruptcy. As part of those transactions, reorganized Syms merged with and into the Company, with the Company as the surviving corporation and successor issuer pursuant to Rule 12g-3 under Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “Exchange Act”). In March 2016, we satisfied our final payment and reserve obligations under the Plan.  In February 2018, the bankruptcy court entered the final decree pursuant to which the chapter 11 cases of the reorganized debtors were closed.

 

Following the bankruptcy, the Company owned commercial real estate and a variety of intellectual property assets focused on the consumer sector. Our business plan originally included the monetization of commercial real estate properties and a condominium the Company owned as of the effective date of the Plan, and the sale or development of 28-42 Trinity Place (77 Greenwich Street) in Lower Manhattan, referred to as “77 Greenwich” and also referred to herein as the “77G Property”). In the initial years following consummation of the Chapter 11 reorganization of Syms, the Company sold the substantial majority of its original properties and began development of 77 Greenwich. We subsequently acquired a newly built 105-unit, 12-story multi-family property located at 237 11th Street in Brooklyn, New York (the “237 11th” or the “237 11th Property”), in May 2018, and, through joint ventures, a 50% interest in a newly constructed 95-unit multi-family property in Brooklyn, New York known as The Berkley in December 2016, and a 10% interest in a 234-unit multi-family property located at 250 North 10th Street (“250 North 10th Street”) acquired in January 2020, also in Brooklyn, New York.  In order to raise capital, we sold our interest in The Berkley in April 2022, and our interest in 250 North 10th Street in February 2023. See “Background of the Transactions – Adverse Developments in the Company’s Business and Markets” below for further discussion.

 

Currently, our largest asset is 77 Greenwich, which is a substantially complete mixed-use project consisting of a 90-unit residential condominium tower, retail space and a New York City elementary school. We also continue to own 237 11th, as well as a legacy Syms property occupied by retail tenants in Paramus, New Jersey (the “Paramus Property”, and together with the 77G Property and the 237 11th Property, individually referred to as a “Property” and collectively, the “Properties”). We also control a variety of intellectual property assets focused on the consumer sector, a legacy of our predecessor Syms, including among others FilenesBasement.com. In addition, we had approximately $305.4 million of federal net operating loss carryforwards (“NOLs”) at September 30, 2023, as well as various state and local NOLs, which can be used to reduce our future taxable income and capital gains.

 

Background of the Transactions

 

Adverse Developments in the Company’s Business and Markets

 

As disclosed in its Exchange Act reports and filings, the Company has experienced a number of challenges over the past several years, in particular as it relates to its two largest assets, 77 Greenwich and 237 11th, including among others the COVID-19 pandemic. In late December 2019, shortly before the pandemic, the Company entered into the CCF (defined below) with the Company Investor, a primary purpose of which was to take advantage of opportunities in the market at that time. The impact of the pandemic and other challenges was exacerbated by the Company’s small size relative to many of its competitors in the New York City real estate market.

 

77 Greenwich

 

The Company’s largest asset, 77 Greenwich, is a condominium development project. This type of asset does not generate profits until the completed condominium units are sold. Generally, the profit is generated from the sale of the last units of the project. The COVID-19 pandemic and related matters, including government actions and supply chain issues, delayed the completion date of 77 Greenwich, resulting in our needing to fund debt service, and condominium related carry costs, through a delayed and longer sellout period. In addition, shifts in residential consumer sentiment and changes to the broader and local economies have had a significant adverse impact on our business. More recently, world events, the economic downturn, regional bank failures, an unprecedented rapid increase in interest rates, tighter lending standards and a corresponding decrease in lending, volatility in stock market valuations, higher levels of inflation, and current financial market challenges have also materially adversely impacted our business. In addition, construction work is ongoing and there continue to be delays and issues the impact of which is not yet known, which may delay final completion. As a result, although we have sold approximately 42.2 % of the condominium units at 77 Greenwich, and the units that remain to be sold are larger, higher floor units, the timing of the sell-out has been materially impeded, resulting in a delayed repayment of the loan under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement (defined below) secured by the 77G Property and significantly greater than anticipated debt service costs associated with the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement and the Mezzanine Loan Agreement (defined below) and a corresponding adverse impact on our financial condition.

 

6

 

 

237 11th

 

Following closing of the acquisition of 237 11th in 2018, we identified water damage and other defects in apartment units and other property at 237 11th resulting from construction defects which we believe were concealed by the prior ownership team and its general contractor. Accordingly, we submitted a notice of claim to our insurance carrier for property damage and business interruption (lost revenue). The insurance carrier subsequently disclaimed coverage for the losses and we filed a complaint against the carrier alleging that it breached the insurance policy by denying coverage. We also filed legal claims against the seller, its parent company, and the general contractor to recover damages arising from the defective construction of the building, including defects that resulted in water damage as well as other defects, and lost rents. In addition, the general contractor impleaded into that litigation several subcontractors who performed work on the property. Management expects to recover some portion of the cost incurred to repair the property, and lost rents, through the litigations and/or settlement negotiations with the seller, its parent company, the general contractor, the subcontractors, and the insurance carrier, although the amount of damages that may be recoverable in litigation and/or potential settlement negotiations are uncertain at this time, as is the timing of receipt of any such payments. The timing of any recovery has been adversely impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, including the resulting backlog in the court system and slowdown in judicial proceedings. We have, from time to time, engaged in mediation with the seller, its parent company, the general contractor, and the third-party defendants impleaded by the general contractor to explore the possibility of settling the case involving those parties, but to date, we have not reached an agreement, and we continue to pursue all legal remedies. We expeditiously and completely corrected all defects, at our expense, incurring significant cash outflows for costs associated with these repairs and remediation, which commenced in September 2019 and were completed by December 31, 2021. In addition, we vacated substantially the entire building resulting in a material reduction in rental revenue for over 2 years. As a result, although the property is now fully leased, the significant increased costs to remediate the defects and the significant lost revenue also had an adverse impact on our financial condition.

 

Liquidity and Going Concern

 

Beginning with its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2020, the Company has disclosed the uncertainty as to its ability to continue as a going concern as a result of the potential inability to pay its outstanding debt as it becomes due, and the Company’s independent auditor’s report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022 included an explanatory paragraph regarding substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern, and that absent additional financing and debt modifications the Company may be unable to remain a going concern. In its most recent Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the quarter ended September 30, 2023, the Company disclosed that its cash and cash equivalents will not be sufficient to fund the Company’s operations, debt service, amortization and maturities and corporate expenses beyond the next few months, unless the Company is able to both extend or refinance or otherwise resolve its maturing debt and also raise additional capital or enter into a strategic transaction.

 

We have generated limited revenues and had negative cash flow from operations since our formation, and the development of the Company’s business plan required substantial capital for new investments and operating expenses. For the first 5-6 years subsequent to the emergence from bankruptcy, the Company was able to successfully execute on its business plan, but the challenges in the credit and equity capital markets in recent years have had an adverse impact not only on its financial condition but also on its stock price and its ability to raise equity capital and property level debt. Because the Company historically generated losses, it was dependent on its ability to raise capital at acceptable prices and rates in order to implement its business plan. As a result, the Company’s cash position has decreased in recent years. While the Company was able to raise capital in recent years through the sale of assets, namely its joint venture interests in The Berkley, which resulted in net proceeds of approximately $8 million, and 250 North 10th, which resulted in net proceeds of approximately $1.2 million, and to a lesser extent through a private placement and rights offering of Common Stock, and additional borrowings under its CCF, the amounts raised have not been sufficient to overcome the challenges associated with the factors above.

 

7

 

 

Uncertainty Over the Company’s Financial Outlook.

 

As of the date hereof, we were in default under (i) the Master Loan Agreement (the “77G Mortgage Loan Agreement”), dated as of October 22, 2021 by and between TPHGreenwich Owner LLC (the “77G Owner”) and MPF Greenwich Lender LLC (as successor-in-interest to Macquarie PF Inc.), as lender and administrative agent (the “77G Mortgage Lender”), (ii) the Mezzanine Loan Agreement (the “Mezzanine Loan Agreement”, and the loan under such agreement, the “Mezzanine Loan”) dated as of December 22, 2020 by and between the Company and TPHS Lender II LLC, an affiliate of the Investor (the “Mezzanine Lender”), and (iii) the Corporate Credit Agreement (the “CCF”) dated as of December 19, 2019 by and between the Company and the Company Investor, as initial lender and as administrative agent. Approximately $197.3 million in the aggregate was outstanding under these agreements as of December 31, 2023, inclusive of approximately $31.7 million of accrued interest.

 

As of December 31, 2023, the Company had approximately $320,000 of unrestricted cash. As such, we do not have sufficient liquidity to repay the indebtedness under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, the Mezzanine Loan Agreement, or the CCF, or to operate for more than the next few months, giving effect to the additional $375,000 expected to be funded pursuant to the additional incremental term loans under our CCF on or about the date of submission of these preliminary consent solicitation materials.

 

The Company has entered into forbearance agreements with each of the lenders under their respective facilities, pursuant to which the lenders agreed to forbear from exercising their rights and remedies under their facilities with respect to the Company and/or its subsidiaries’ failure to make payments or achieve other milestones required thereunder (the “Forbearance Periods”). Most recently, the 77G Mortgage Lender notified the Company that it had elected to extend the Forbearance Period from December 20, 2023 to January 31, 2024, provided that such extended period shall only apply to the extent the Company has submitted on or prior to January 5 the preliminary consent solicitation with the SEC for stockholder approval of the transaction between the Company and Investor and provided the lender with reasonably acceptable evidence; the 77G Mezzanine Lender notified the Company that it had elected to extend the Forbearance Period from December 31, 2023 to January 31, 2024, provided that such extended period shall only apply to the extent the 77G Mortgage Lender is forbearing pursuant to the terms of its December 20 extension letter, and, for so long as the Forbearance Period with the 77G Mezzanine Lender is continuing, the lender under the CCF agreed that no event of default under the CCF shall be deemed to have occurred as a result of the forbearance defaults. The lenders will have the right, following the expiration of their respective Forbearance Periods, to exercise their rights and remedies with respect to such defaults. In such event, the lenders could demand immediate repayment of the outstanding borrowings, terminate the facilities and seek to foreclose on the assets securing the loans, among other remedies. The exercise of any such rights and remedies by the 77G Mortgage Lender or the Mezzanine Lender may also trigger claims by purchasers under signed residential unit contracts at 77 Greenwich to rescind such contracts or an order from the New York State Department of Law to make an offer of rescission.

 

Background to the Transactions Contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement

 

As previously disclosed, Company management has engaged from time to time with various parties who have expressed interest in the Company’s assets and attributes and the Company has considered potential strategic transactions including acquisitions, dispositions, financings and refinancings.

 

On September 9, 2022, the Company announced that it had entered into an exclusivity period with an unaffiliated asset manager during which time the parties attempted to negotiate and execute binding documents which would call for refinancings at improved economic terms reflecting the reduced risk profile of the Company, and the availability of new cash and financings for the purpose of making a substantial investment in one or more mutually approved new business opportunities that may activate certain Company attributes. The exclusivity period ended and negotiations did not result in a transaction. The Company continued to explore potential strategic transactions.

 

On February 7, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the board observer appointed in accordance with the terms of the CCF by the Company Investor, as lender under the CCF (the “Board Observer”), members of Company management and a representative of Kramer Levin Naftalis & Frankel LLP, the Company’s corporate legal counsel (“Kramer Levin”) were present. At the meeting, management discussed with the Board the cash position of the Company, and that the Company was pursuing a refinancing of 237 11th despite the challenges in the current debt markets, the upcoming maturity of the loan under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement in October 2023, and that an extension seemed unlikely, and also the continuing efforts to sell the Paramus Property, which had been under contract for sale but the agreement was terminated by the buyer in January 2023. Management also provided the Board with an update on the Company’s pursuit of a strategic transaction and related discussions with third parties.

  

8

 

 

On February 27, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of Company management and a representative of Kramer Levin were present. At the meeting, management discussions with the Board included the Company’s liquidity, upcoming cash payment obligations and discussions with lenders.

 

On March 14, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the Board Observer, members of Company management and a representative of Kramer Levin were present. At the meeting, management discussed with the Board, among other topics, near term actions that could be taken to preserve the maximum amount of cash in order to provide the maximum amount of time for a strategic process, as well as further discussion regarding the potential sale of 237 11th and the Paramus Property.

 

On March 20, 2023 and March 21, 2023, the Company entered into engagement letters with Houlihan Lokey Capital, Inc. (“Houlihan Lokey”) and Ackman-Ziff Real Estate Capital (“AZ” and together with Houlihan Lokey, the “Advisors”), pursuant to which the Company engaged the Advisors to provide financial advisory and/or placement agent services to the Company in connection with its strategic review process and to assist the Company in identifying and evaluating potential strategic alternatives, including securing an equity and/or debt financing of the Company, refinancing of existing debt, and/or a sale or merger or reverse merger of the Company. At that time the Company was also in discussions with its lenders regarding the deferment of upcoming interest, amortization and other payment obligations for the period ending March 31, 2023 and going forward, and was exploring a refinancing of the debt in respect of 237 11th.

 

Our Advisors conducted an extensive strategic review process in the spring and through the summer of 2023, which included reaching out to more than 100 potential counterparties, which resulted in the Company executing non-disclosure agreements and having varying levels of information exchange with 35 parties. The outreach process is ongoing.

 

On April 20, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the Board Observer and an additional principal of an affiliate of the Investor, members of Company management, a representative of Kramer Levin and representatives from the Advisors were present. Discussion at the meeting included matters relating to the strategic process, cash preservation and various potential paths that could evolve depending on actions taken by the 77G Mortgage Lender, cash needs and the outcome of the strategic process.

 

On April 21, 2023, the Company entered into a sixth amendment to the CCF, pursuant to which the Company reached agreement with Investor, as the lender under the CCF, regarding, among other things, the deferment of cash interest payments and a $7 million prepayment until August 31, 2023, subject to extension in certain circumstances, which also provided that the Company will enter into a strategic transaction that results in the repayment of the CCF or prepay the CCF by $5 million from equity proceeds by such date. Under the amendment, the Company Investor was also granted the right to appoint an independent director to the Company’s Board of Directors, in addition to its existing right to appoint a director or Board observer.

 

On April 27, 2023, the Paramus Owner entered into an amendment to the Paramus Credit Agreement (defined below) extending the maturity date to March 22, 2024 and reducing the interest rate to 2.5% until such date.

 

On May 10, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the Board Observer and a principal of an affiliate of the Investor, management and representatives from the Advisors and Kramer Levin were present. Management reviewed the Company’s cash situation, which continued to be very tight, as well as the upcoming maturities of the 237 11th mortgage and mezzanine debt, noting, among other things, the Company’s ability to extend the terms of each upon purchase of a new interest rate cap, which would require significant cash outlays; the refinancing market and the status of recent discussions; and longer-term prospects for monetization of the asset. The Board and those present then reviewed generally other items to be addressed in the coming months, including monthly interest payments under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, recent market dynamics, and discussions among the 77G Mortgage Lender, the Mezzanine Lender and management. A wide-ranging discussion was held including the Board and those present on matters related to the 77G Property and the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement. The Board and those present also reviewed the strategic process, the Company’s liquidity, and related matters, including next steps in the strategic process as well as all potential alternatives, including a bankruptcy filing, depending on the evolution of the process and interactions with the 77G Mortgage Lender.

 

9

 

 

On May 23, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of Company management and representatives from the Advisors, Kramer Levin and Morris, Nichols, Arsht & Tunnell LLP, the Company’s Delaware legal counsel (“MNAT”) were present. Management provided the Board with an update on the Company’s near-term liquidity constraints, the status of 237 11th refinancing discussions (which debt would mature in early July 2023) and a proposal received from the Investor for incremental financing. The Board and those present then reviewed recent developments in the strategic process, potential paths that could evolve with respect to the strategic process, and the relative advantages and disadvantages of proposals from investors, including the Investor.

 

On June 6, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of Company management and representatives from Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. At the meeting, management reviewed the status of negotiations on the upsizing of the CCF and a representative from Kramer Levin discussed the terms of the potential extension. Counsel also reviewed with the Board the Company’s liquidity and options as it related to the current proposed amendment and upsizing, which principally were to obtain a $3,000,000 incremental financing and gain time to complete the strategic process and/or negotiate a holistic resolution with the lender, as absent a strategic transaction or timely holistic resolution with the lender, a Chapter 11 filing may be needed to implement a restructuring.

 

On June 9, 2023, the Company entered into a seventh amendment to the CCF, which provided, among other things, that (i) the CCF be increased by up to $5,000,000, with $3,000,000 to be used for general corporate purposes and certain other items if applicable, and up to $2,000,000 to be used in connection with the extension of the loans in respect of the 237 11th Property, including the purchase of an interest rate cap, (ii) certain covenants and other terms of the CCF were revised, including that on or before June 30, 2023, the Company would meet with the Company Investor to review the results of the Company’s strategic process, endeavor in good faith to establish mutually acceptable next steps, and provide copies of written term sheets received from participants in the strategic process, including at least one that addresses repayment or purchase of the Loan; and the removal of the ability of the Company to incur certain types of previously permitted debt and make previously permitted investments and other restricted payments

 

On June 27, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the Board Observer, members of Company management, an additional principal of an affiliate of the Investor, and representatives from the Advisors, Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. A representative from Houlihan Lokey provided the Board with an overview of the investors to whom Houlihan Lokey had reached out, the process and the goals, including entering into exclusivity with an investor in the near-term. A representative from Houlihan Lokey also provided an overview of the proposed terms, goals and plans of potential investors, each of whom had provided terms and a business plan, in varying levels of detail, and which terms required certain third-party concessions and modifications of certain loan agreements that the lenders indicated they would be unable or unwilling to make. Following the conclusion of the presentation, the Board Observer noted that the Company Investor or its affiliates would present its own proposal as well. Board members and those present at the meeting discussed the existing proposals before the Company and the Board, the pending proposal from the Investor, and the impact of a Chapter 11 process in terms of costs and expenses, time, and on the sales of condominiums at 77 Greenwich, among other things.

 

In July 2023, the Company exercised its first extension option for the loans in respect of the 237 11th Property which extended the maturity date of the debt to July 2024.

 

On July 17, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the members of Company management, representatives from the Advisors, Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. A representative from Houlihan Lokey provided an update on the strategic process, including that the parties were exchanging drafts of a term sheet for a potential strategic transaction with an unaffiliated asset manager with investment expertise in, among other things, real estate in the public and private markets (the “Potential Strategic Party”) and reviewed with the Board the term sheet provided by an affiliate of the Investor and whether the proposal would maximize the value of the Company’s assets. The Board and those present then held a wide-ranging discussion on the strategic process, the Company’s liquidity, alternatives and related matters. A representative from MNAT described the potential paths from a bankruptcy perspective. Management also provided an update on the Company’s liquidity position, potential variances and upcoming payments, including the August 31, 2023 bullet payment due to be made to the Company Investor, as lender under the CCF and that the coming weeks would be critical for finalizing a path.

 

10

 

 

On August 9, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which the members of Company management, representatives from the Advisors, Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. Management summarized for the Board discussions to date with the Investor regarding a potential transaction, and that the current proposal consisted of the transfer of 77 Greenwich and 237 11th, and all associated liabilities and the CCF, into an entity that would be controlled by the Investor and managed by the Company for a fee. This structure would allow the Company to transact with a new investor with less impact from the legacy liabilities and assets. Management noted that the Investor and its counsel were preparing a term sheet to reflect the proposal, and also that a term sheet with the Potential Strategic Party was close to being finalized. Management also outlined the sequencing of certain key upcoming dates and steps, which consisted of agreeing and executing term sheets with the Investor and the Potential Strategic Party, while parallel processing forbearance agreements with the CCF Lender and Mezzanine Lender by August 31, 2023 and then, the 77G Mortgage Lender by September 1, 2023. Management noted, however, the Company needed to also begin preparations for a potential bankruptcy filing in case it was required. A representative from Houlihan Lokey provided a summary of the proposed transactions in more detail. The Board and those present then held a discussion on the strategic process, the Company’s liquidity, timing process and the potential alternatives and related matters. A representative from MNAT then reviewed with the Board certain considerations from a bankruptcy perspective.

 

On August 24, 2023, the Company entered into term sheets with the Potential Strategic Party, and an affiliate of the Company Investor and Mezzanine Lender, each providing for a 30-day exclusivity period, subject to customary terms and conditions, for purposes of finalizing due diligence and negotiating definitive documentation for investments in the Company and a modification of the Company’s debt, respectively. Also on August 24, 2023, the Company entered into the Mezzanine Loan Forbearance Agreement and CCF Forbearance Agreement (each as defined below), pursuant to which each of the Mezzanine Lender and Investor, as lender under the CCF, agreed to forbear from exercising its respective rights and remedies with respect to certain specified defaults for the related forbearance period ending on December 31, 2023, unless earlier terminated as a result of certain termination events, including the consummation of a strategic transaction or the Potential Strategic Party notifying the Company in writing that it is no longer pursuing a transaction with the Company and it is not replaced by a third party pursuing a substantially similar transaction within thirty days.

 

On September 6, 2023 and effective as of September 1, 2023, the Company and the 77G Owner entered into the 77G Mortgage Loan Forbearance Agreement, for the purpose of providing additional time for the Company to pursue a potential strategic transaction as described above, pursuant to which the 77G Mortgage Lender agreed to forbear from exercising its rights and remedies during the Forbearance Period until November 15, 2023, as defined below, with respect to any failure by the 77G Owner, as borrower, to (i) make payments under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, including, without limitation, interest payments due on September 1, 2023 and principal and interest payments due at maturity and (ii) achieve any Milestone Construction Hurdles or to satisfy the Quarterly Sales Hurdle (each as defined in the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement) or make the related prepayment as and when required.

 

On October 6, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of management and a representative from Kramer Levin were present. Management provided an update on the status of the transactions with the Potential Strategic Party and the Investor and progress to date in each, including among other things as it related to draft transaction documents, tax analysis, structuring and due diligence with each counterparty and their advisors. The key transaction documents for the Investor transaction were all in process, and Kramer Levin had provided initial drafts of certain documents in respect of the transaction with the Potential Strategic Party which had indicated that initial drafts of the documents which they were preparing were in process along with comments to documents provided by Kramer Levin. Management also reviewed key business terms as well as structuring and tax matters and potential revisions to the proposed transactions, which were envisioned to continue to materially reflect the economic terms previously agreed. Management also updated the Board on the status of discussions with the 77G Mortgage Lender as it related to a restructuring of the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement.

 

On October 20, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of management and a representative from Kramer Levin were present. Management provided an update to the Board regarding the status of documentation, including as it related to the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, and the Investor. Management also noted that, while progress had been made with the Potential Strategic Party, recent communications with Houlihan Lokey and a lack of documents suggested hesitancy to proceed with the transaction, although the principals had not contacted management directly or sent a written communication. Management outlined for the Board potential scenarios, including transactions proceeding with the Investor and the 77G Mortgage Lender, potential additional investors and the interplay with the Company’s liquidity.

 

11

 

 

In late October 2023, the Potential Strategic Party subsequently indicated its intent to not proceed with the transaction on the terms provided under the term sheet. Although discussions continued, on December 22, 2023, the Potential Strategic Party indicated its intent not to proceed with any transaction.

 

On November 8, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of management and representatives of Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. Management provided an update on discussions with the Potential Strategic Party, as well as discussions with the Investor about potential alternatives, including a bankruptcy filing and an investment by Investor, and the potential terms of such an investment and the amount and use of proceeds, including working capital and transaction expenses, which would necessitate agreement with the Company’s advisors as to discounts. Management next provided an update on discussions with the 77G Mortgage Lender and affiliates of the Mezzanine Lender regarding a potential restructuring of the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, which were contemplated to include among other things an extension and paydown of the loan, with funds to be provided by the Mezzanine Lender or an affiliate. Management and counsel next provided an update on the process and timing of the foregoing, including shareholder approval of any necessary items.

 

On November 15, 2023, the Forbearance Period under the 77G Mortgage Loan Forbearance Agreement terminated in accordance with the terms of the agreement.

 

On November 26, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of management and representatives from Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. Management provided an update to the Board regarding the status of discussions with the 77G Mortgage Lender and affiliates of Investor, including proposed non-binding terms of a proposal to restructure the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, as well as a reinstatement and extension of the Forbearance Period. Management also provided an update on the status of discussions with the Investor, including among other things contemplated near-term additional lendings to provide $750,000 of incremental capital to the Company, in two stages based on the progress of documentation and related matters. Management also reviewed the impact to the Company if the investment and related transactions were completed, including an additional amount of time and an improved structure for an additional investor to invest in the Company, although there was no assurance one would be identified or if identified a transaction would be completed. Counsel reviewed alternative scenarios, including among others a bankruptcy filing.

 

On November 28, 2023, the Company, the 77G Owner, as borrower, and the 77G Mortgage Lender entered into an agreement pursuant to which, among other things, the 77G Mortgage Lender agreed to reinstate the Forbearance Period effective as of November 15, 2023 and extend the Forbearance Period to December 20, 2023, as such date may be further extended by the Mortgage Lender in its sole discretion by written notice to the Mortgage Borrower. In connection with certain proposed transactions described in more detail below, the parties also agreed to non-binding terms which contemplate certain amendments to the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, including among others, an extension of the maturity date by two years, subject to an extension by an additional one-year period if certain conditions are satisfied, as well as a material payment on the outstanding balance of the loan, which would be funded by affiliates of the Company Investor.

 

The Company continued discussions with affiliates of the Investor and on December 1, 2023, the Company entered into a non-binding term sheet with the Company Investor and JV Investor pursuant to which the Company Investor would commit to purchase 25,112,245 newly-issued shares of Common Stock at a price of $0.30 per share, such that immediately following such purchase, the Company Investor would own 40.6% of the total number of outstanding shares of Common Stock in the Company, including its 750,000 existing-owned shares, and the amounts paid by the Company Investor for such equity interests would be used to restructure certain debts of the Company and its affiliates and support the continuation of the Company’s and its affiliates’ operations pursuant to a budget and business plan approved by the Investor.

 

On December 1, 2023, the Company entered into an eighth amendment to the CCF, which provided among other things for the provision of incremental term loan advances under the CCF in the amount of $750,000, with the first $375,000 being provided upon execution of the amendment and the second $375,000 to be provided upon and subject to Board approval of definitive agreements in respect of certain proposed transactions with the Company Investor and/or its affiliates, on the terms set forth in the non-binding term sheet and the filing of preliminary materials with the SEC for the solicitation of the vote or consent of the Company’s stockholders, if required. The amendment also amended the Company’s CCF Forbearance Agreement with respect to certain additional defaults in respect of which the lender is forbearing. The terms of the CCF Forbearance Agreement were otherwise unchanged.

 

On December 9, 2023, Kramer Levin delivered a draft of the stock purchase agreement to Dechert LLP, corporate legal counsel for the Investor (“Dechert”). The parties continued to negotiate the terms and exchange drafts of the Stock Purchase Agreement and other agreements, with respect to which drafts had been previously exchanged.

 

On December 20, 2023, the 77G Mortgage Lender notified the Company that it had elected to extend the Forbearance Period from December 20, 2023 to January 31, 2024, provided that such extended period shall only apply to the extent the Company has submitted on or prior to January 5 the preliminary consent solicitation with the SEC for stockholder approval of the transaction between the Company and Investor and provided the lender with reasonably acceptable evidence. 

 

On December 22, 2023, the 77G Mezzanine Lender notified the Company that it had elected to extend the Forbearance Period from December 31, 2023 to January 31, 2024, provided that such extended period shall only apply to the extent the 77G Mortgage Lender is forbearing pursuant to the terms of the December 20 extension letter, and, for so long as the forbearance period is continuing, the lender under the CCF agreed that no Event of Default under the CCF shall be deemed to have occurred as a result of the forbearance defaults. 

 

On December 22, 2023, the Board held a meeting at which members of management, Houlihan Lokey, Kramer Levin and MNAT were present. Management provided an update on the status of discussions with the 77G Mortgage Lender, including documentation with respect to restructuring of the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, which had been negotiated with the input of the Mezzanine Lender and its affiliates in conjunction with the proposed transactions with the Investor and was contemplated to be entered by the applicable parties upon closing of such transactions. Counsel reviewed the proposed key documents with the Investor to implement the transactions, including a stock purchase agreement, a joint venture agreement and an asset management agreement, the status of negotiations and anticipated timing and process. Management also provided an update on the Company’s liquidity and related matters.

 

12

 

 

On January 2, 2024, the Board met to consider and discuss the proposed Transactions with the Investor, which was attended by representatives of each of the Company’s management team, Kramer Levin, the Advisors and MNAT. Representatives from Houlihan Lokey provided a summary of the key business terms of the Transactions and Counsel reviewed with the Board the provisions of the proposed Stock Purchase Agreement, JV Operating Agreement and other principal transaction documents, copies of which had been previously provided to the Board. Management and the advisors discussed the principal considerations and key strategic rationales with respect to the proposed transaction. Representatives from Houlihan Lokey provided an overview of the activities that the Company engaged in since March 2023 in connection with identifying a potential strategic transaction. Counsel discussed with the Board their fiduciary duties under applicable law. Following these discussions, the Board reviewed and discussed the transaction, the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, JV Operating Agreement and related agreements and matters, with counsel and the Advisors.

 

On January 3, 2024, January 4, 2024 and January 5, 2024, the Board held meetings that were also attended by representatives of each of the Company’s management team, the Advisors and counsel, during which the Board further considered and discussed the proposed Transactions with the Investor and related matters, and counsel provided updates on the status of the transaction documents.

 

On January 5, 2024, the Board unanimously approved (with Matthew Messinger abstaining) the Stock Purchase Agreement and related agreements and the Transactions, and unanimously determined (with Matthew Messinger abstaining), that the proposed Transactions are expedient and for the best interests of the Company, and advisable, fair to and in the best interests of its stockholders, and recommended the approval and adoption of the Stockholder Proposals by the Company’s stockholders.

 

As of January 5, 2024, the Company and Investor entered into the Stock Purchase Agreement.

 

Reasons for the Transactions

 

The Board, in evaluating the Transactions, consulted with its legal counsel, financial and other advisors. In reaching its resolution on January 5, 2024, (i) that the Stock Purchase Agreement and the Transactions are expedient and for the best interests of the Company, and advisable, fair to and in the best interests of its stockholders, (ii) to approve the Stock Purchase Agreement and the Transactions and declare their advisability and (iii) to recommend that the Company’s stockholders approve the Stockholder Proposals, the Board considered and evaluated a number of factors, including, but not limited to, the factors discussed below.

 

In approving the Transactions, the Board determined not to obtain a fairness opinion. The officers and directors of the Company concluded that their experience and background, together with the experience of the Advisors, and the lack of alternatives available to the Company other than a bankruptcy filing or similar outcome, enabled them to make the necessary analyses and determinations regarding the proposed Transactions.

 

13

 

 

The Board considered a number of factors pertaining to the Transactions as generally supporting its decision to enter into the Stock Purchase Agreement and the Transactions, including but not limited to, the following material factors: (i) the Company’s current and historical business, financial condition, results of operations, assets and prospects; (ii) the Company’s limited amount of unrestricted cash and liquidity, and that in the absence of a transaction that provides the Company with additional capital, the Company will have insufficient cash and liquidity to service its debt and pay operating expenses and other obligations beyond the next few months; (iii) the fact that the Company is currently in default of covenants under the 77G Mortgage Loan Agreement, Mezzanine Loan and CCF, and without additional forbearances or amendments of those facilities, outstanding borrowings thereunder are or may become due and payable and the lenders will be able to exercise remedies under those facilities; (iv) the extensive process the Company undertook, with the assistance of its Advisors and legal advisors, to evaluate its strategic alternatives; (v) that the terms of the Stock Purchase Agreement are the result of arm’s-length negotiations conducted by the Company with the assistance of financial and legal advisors; (vi) the fact that the purchase price of the Investor Shares is at a significant premium to the Company’s stock price; (vii) the fact that, at the Closing, a newly formed wholly-owned subsidiary of the Company (the “TPH Manager”) will enter into an asset management agreement with the JV (the “Asset Management Agreement”), and the TPH Manager will receive an annual management fee equal to the greater of $400,000 or 1.25% of the outstanding principal balance under the CCF and Mezzanine Loan (including any future fundings made by the applicable lender under the CCF and the Mezzanine Loan), initially expected to be approximately $1.2 million per year; (viii) the fact that in conjunction with the Transactions, the loan under the 77G Mortgage Agreement is anticipated to be extended and approximately $20 million funded by Investor or its affiliates to pay down accrued interest, fund reserves and rebalance the loan for carry costs and to pay transaction costs related to the 77G Mortgage Agreement; (ix) the fact that representations and warranties will not survive the Closing, and Investor’s only recourse for claims as a result of a breach of representations or warranties will be under a buyer-side representation and warranty insurance policy issued to Investor, if it elects to obtain such insurance; (x) the improved structure for a new investor to invest in the Company, which is less complex as a result of the real estate assets and substantially all liabilities being off-balance sheet; (xi) the fact that the transaction structure is anticipated to permit the Company to preserve its substantial net operating losses; (xii) the fact that the Transaction allows the Company to preserve the value in its tangible assets, including 237 11th and 77 Greenwich, relative to the anticipated impact if the Company were to file for bankruptcy; (xiii) the fact that the JV Investor is not permitted to call capital in the form of equity capital contributions under the JV Operating Agreement for the first year, except in limited circumstances (as a capital call funded in its entirety by the JV Investor without a pro rata contribution by the Company would result in a dilution of the Company’s interest in the JV); and (xiv) the Company’s limited alternatives if the Company does not consummate the Transactions, including but not limited to, filing for bankruptcy protection which would likely result in holders of the Company’s Common Stock receiving little or no value on account of their shares.

 

The Board identified and considered the following factors and risks as weighing negatively against pursuing the Transactions, although not weighted or in any order of significance: (i) the risks and uncertainties of consummating the Transactions; (ii) the extensive representations and warranties and covenants of the Company and conditions to Closing under the Stock Purchase Agreement, including, among others, stockholder approval, the consents of the lenders under the 237 11th facilities and the Investor obtaining a buyer-side representation and warranty insurance policy in form and substance acceptable to it prior to Closing; (iii) the limited recourse of the Company under the Stock Purchase Agreement for breaches by the Investor, including no right to specific performance, the creditworthiness of Investor, and the capping of damages at $500,000; (iv) the fact that Investor would control the borrowers under the Company’s current loan agreements, including loan agreements under which Investor or its affiliates are lenders, and accordingly conflicts of interest could arise; (v) the fact that under the Asset Management Agreement, the TPH Manager can be terminated as asset manager with or without cause; and (vi) the risk that the TPH Manager is terminated as asset manager for cause under the Asset Management Agreement, in which case the Company would not receive distributions, if any, on account of its interests in the JV.

 

The Board has concluded that the potential benefits that it expected the Company and its stockholders to achieve as a result of the Transactions and the consequences to the Company and its stockholders if the proposed Transactions are not completed, outweigh the potentially negative factors associated with the proposed Transactions. Accordingly, the Board unanimously determined (with Matthew Messinger abstaining) that the Stock Purchase Agreement and the Transactions, were expedient and for the best interests of the Company, and advisable, fair to and in the best interests of its stockholders.

 

Impact of Stockholder Consent

 

We have a limited amount of unrestricted cash and liquidity available for working capital and our cash needs are significant, including near term debt maturities and other payment obligations. As previously reported, at October 31, 2023, the Company’s cash and cash equivalents totaled approximately $583,000. While the Company was advanced $375,000 pursuant to incremental term loans under the CCF on December 1, 2023, and anticipates another $375,000 will be advanced pursuant to additional incremental term loans on or about the date on which this Consent Solicitation Statement is filed with the SEC, given the Company’s limited operating cash position, if the Company’s stockholders do not approve the Stockholder Proposals and the Company is unable to reach an agreement with the lenders with respect to additional forbearance, restructuring and extension of the facilities, the Company’s cash and cash equivalents will not be sufficient to fund the Company’s operations, debt service, amortization and maturities and corporate expenses beyond the next few months. The Company expects that under the circumstances, it will likely not be possible to consummate another strategic transaction or otherwise obtain capital sufficient to meet the operating costs described above.

 

14

 

 

As a result, if the Company’s stockholders do not approve the Stockholder Proposals, or the Transactions are not completed for any other reason, the Company expects to file for bankruptcy protection within a short period of time, given the Company’s limited cash. In addition, the Forbearance Periods with its lenders end no later than January 31, 2024, unless further extended. As a result, the Company will likely have no viable alternative but to file for bankruptcy protection should the Transactions not be completed. Given the Company’s outstanding indebtedness and the costs of a bankruptcy process, the Company believes that its stockholders would receive substantially no value on account of their shares of stock in that situation.

 

Even if the Company’s stockholders approve the Stockholder Proposals, there can be no assurances that other Closing conditions are satisfied or waived, or that third parties will not take actions that may delay or derail the Transactions. Defending against any third-party actions could further reduce the liquidity or assets of the Company needed to conduct or consummate the proposed Transactions or operate its business, and accelerate a Chapter 11 filing or other restructuring of the Company’s liabilities. There can be no assurance that the proposed Transactions will proceed successfully or on time or ultimately be consummated as a result of the failure of Closing conditions to be satisfied or waived, possible future litigation or other adverse events.

 

If the Transactions are completed, the Company will receive approximately $7,500,000 of gross proceeds on account of the sale of shares of Common Stock in the Investment Transaction. Approximately $2,966,476 of these proceeds are expected to go toward transaction expenses, reflecting a significant and approximately 60% discount to amounts claimed by the Company’s Advisors and legal counsel, with the remainder to be used by the Company in the ordinary course for its operating expenses, including payment of critical aging payables. While the length of time the proceeds will last is uncertain, taking in account payment of outstanding operating expenses, the Company believes that absent an additional cash infusion the proceeds will allow the Company to continue to operate for 6-8 months. During this time, the Company and its Advisors plan to continue to explore potential strategic alternatives. We do not currently have any letters of intent or binding agreements for any such strategic alternatives. While there can be no assurances that any strategic alternative will be identified, or that, if identified, any transaction agreements will be entered into or will close, the structure of the Transactions both gives the Company the financial resources to continue exploring alternatives for a period of time and also segregates the Company’s principal liabilities and real estate assets in the JV, which the Company believes will enhance its attractiveness for a potential investment into the Company by a third party.

 

For the reasons stated above, the Board believes that entering into the Transactions provides the best available opportunity for the Company to receive a new investment from a strategic counterparty. And, while the outcome is uncertain and the Company may ultimately not enter into a transaction with a third party, and if it does, there is no assurance that any such transaction will result in any meaningful benefit to the stockholders of the Company, the alternative is likely a near-term bankruptcy, liquidation, dissolution or other restructuring of its liabilities.

 

Notwithstanding the approval of the Stockholder Proposals by the stockholders of the Company, the Board may abandon the Stockholder Proposals at any time prior to the consummation of the Transactions without any further action by the Company’s stockholders, subject to the rights, if any, of third parties under any contract relating thereto.

 

Certain Information About Investor

 

The Investor is an affiliate of Davidson Kempner Capital Management LP, a global institutional investment management firm headquartered in New York with more than $33 billion in assets under management. The firm and its affiliates are registered with or regulated by the SEC, the United Kingdom Financial Conduct Authority, and the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission. The Company Investor is also the lender under the CCF and the Investor is an affiliate of the lender under the Mezzanine Loan.

 

15

 

 

JOINT VENTURE PROPOSAL

 

Stockholders are being asked to adopt resolutions authorizing the (i) JV Operating Agreement, pursuant to which the JV Investor will be appointed the initial manager of the JV, which JV will continue to own all of the real property assets of the Company upon the consummation of Transactions, (ii) Stock Purchase Agreement, pursuant to which the Company Investor will be issued the Investor Shares in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Stock Purchase Agreement, and (iii) Transactions contemplated thereby, which Transactions may be deemed to be a sale, lease or exchange of all or substantially all of the property and assets of the Company within the meaning of Section 271 of the DGCL.

 

JV Operating Agreement

 

At the Closing, the Company and the JV Investor will enter into the JV Operating Agreement, with the Company owning 95% of the ownership interests in the JV and the JV Investor owning 5% of the ownership interests in the JV.

 

Under the terms of the JV Operating Agreement, the JV Investor may call upon each member to make additional capital contributions after the Closing Date; provided however, so long as the TPH Manager has not been terminated for “Cause” under the Asset Management Agreement, the JV Investor agrees not to call for additional capital in the form of equity capital contributions prior to the 1-year anniversary of the Closing. If a member fails to fund its pro rata share of any capital contribution required by the JV Investor, the other member may elect to make an inter-member loan or make a capital contribution on behalf of the non-funding member and dilute the non-funding member’s percentage interest on a dollar-for-dollar basis. The JV Investor, in its capacity as manager of the JV, has the right, in its sole discretion, to cause the JV or any subsidiary to borrow from Investor, its affiliate or any third party, any amounts that would otherwise be covered by a capital call to the members, on then-current market terms. The Company will be liable for any loss or expense incurred by the JV Investor or its affiliates under any non-recourse carveout guaranty or environmental indemnity to a third-party lender, to the extent such loss or expense was caused solely by the acts or omissions of the Company, the TPH Manager or any of their respective affiliates without the prior written consent of the JV Investor or any affiliate of the JV Investor.
